correctness.rationale: Core triggers implement ADR-0001: three date types, −6h behavioral rule, provenance, and tiered TZ resolution. A WHOOP backfill script bug can leave user_tz_log under-populated and some non-cycle events fall back to ET on travel days. Minor inconsistencies in tz_source labeling and cycle-pick logic for whoop_journal remain.

robustness.rationale: Edge cases are handled: DST artifacts filtered in WHOOP auto-fill, transition-day flag, tz_log_gaps canary. Most NULL and degenerate inputs are guarded. Gaps remain around midday events when user_tz_log is missing and a small whoop_journal dedup choice.

scalability.rationale: Derivation centralized in a STABLE function; BEFORE triggers write in-place with indexes on behavioral_date. tz_for_instant has an index-backed lookup. Views use lateral picks and aggregation efficiently at n=1 scale and beyond.

idiomaticness.rationale: Good use of AT TIME ZONE, BEFORE triggers, function composition, provenance. Minor bespoke bits (interval_to_tzd) and a few semantic labeling choices keep it just shy of perfect.

response.summary:
Overall, the implementation closely matches ADR-0001. The −6h behavioral-day rule is applied consistently in pds.derive_onyx_dates across WHOOP, Garmin, Spotify, and Onyx-owned sources. Tiered TZ resolution is realized: source-provided offsets first, then a WHOOP cycle anchor when the instant falls inside [start_time, end_time), and finally pds.user_tz_log with a clearly flagged ET fallback. Provenance is carried via onyx_tz_source, transition days are flagged on cycles, and a tz_log_gaps view elevates missing travel-log entries while distinguishing DST artifacts.

Two issues merit attention. First, whoop_tz_backfill.py has a NameError that prevents it from inserting auto-inferred user_tz_log rows; without these, midday events outside the sleep window can default to ET on travel days until manual/GPS rows are present. Second, the whoop_journal trigger picks the earliest cycle on a behavioral day, not the longest, which can mis-attribute when multiple cycles share a day.

Smaller inconsistencies: garmin_hrv’s no-instant fallback labels tz_source as default_et_fallback while copying a watch-local calendar_date into onyx_et_date/local/behavioral, which is semantically misleading. Cycle-anchor lookup is limited to the sleep window; this is by design but increases reliance on user_tz_log for waking events.

Top 3 fixes: (1) Fix whoop_tz_backfill NameError and add unit smoke to ensure proposals print/insert. (2) Change whoop_journal cycle selection to “longest cycle” to match the behavioral matrix. (3) Adjust garmin_hrv fallback provenance to source_field (or a dedicated label) and document its semantics, or prefer cycle/user_tz_log where possible.

findings.title: whoop_tz_backfill crashes due to undefined variable in notes
findings.description: The script references log_offset_str in the notes f-string but never defines it, raising a NameError and preventing any proposals/inserts.
findings.evidence: notes: (f"whoop-auto: cycle {c['cycle_id']} offset {whoop_offset_str}; ...; log was {log_tz} ({log_offset_str})") — log_offset_str is not defined.
findings.recommendation: Replace log_offset_str with log_offset_at_start (and/or include end) or remove the parenthetical. Add a quick smoke test to ensure proposals list without exception.

findings.title: whoop_journal picks earliest cycle; should pick the longest
findings.description: When multiple cycles share a behavioral day, the trigger selects ORDER BY start_time LIMIT 1, which can choose an arrival nap over the main sleep.
findings.evidence: SELECT wc.start_time, wc.timezone_offset ... ORDER BY wc.start_time LIMIT 1;
findings.recommendation: Align with the behavioral matrix: ORDER BY (end_time - start_time) DESC NULLS LAST, start_time DESC LIMIT 1 to pick the longest cycle.

findings.title: garmin_hrv fallback labels and ET-date semantics are misleading
findings.description: When start_timestamp is NULL, onyx_et_date/local/behavioral are set to a watch-local calendar_date, but onyx_tz_source is set to 'default_et_fallback'. This implies ET-derived fallback yet copies a non-ET date.
findings.recommendation: Use a clearer provenance ('source_field' or a dedicated 'garmin_calendar_date') and document that the date is Garmin’s wake-day. Optionally, prefer cycle/user_tz_log to establish local/behavioral and compute ET via noon-ET projection when feasible.

findings.title: Cycle-anchor limited to sleep window increases ET fallbacks by day
findings.description: pds.cycle_offset_for_instant returns an offset only inside [start_time, end_time). Midday events rely entirely on user_tz_log; if not populated (e.g., WHOOP auto-fill bug or no GPS), events default to ET on travel days.
findings.evidence: WHERE ts >= start_time AND (end_time IS NULL OR ts < end_time)
findings.recommendation: Consider a wider anchor (e.g., map instants to the nearest cycle by time with a capped window) or ensure auto-population (WHOOP/GPS) reliably covers waking hours; at minimum, document the tradeoff.

findings.title: Missing explicit index to support cycle_offset_for_instant lookups
findings.description: cycle_offset_for_instant scans whoop_cycles by start_time with ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1. No index is created in this bundle to guarantee efficient access.
findings.evidence: SELECT timezone_offset FROM pds.whoop_cycles WHERE ts >= start_time ... ORDER BY start_time DESC LIMIT 1;
findings.recommendation: Ensure an index exists on pds.whoop_cycles(start_time) (and ideally on (start_time, end_time)) to keep Tier-2 lookups O(log n) as data grows.

findings.title: gps_tz_backfill may propose noisy same-offset IANA names intermittently
findings.description: Same-offset filter is present, but last_proposed_tz dedup relies on previous inference only; an isolated same-offset activity flanked by gaps could still propose redundant IANA if the offset check ever fails (e.g., rare historical TZ rule anomalies).
findings.evidence: if off_inf == off_log: continue; ... if inferred == last_proposed_tz: continue;
findings.recommendation: Also dedup against the most recent existing user_tz_log row at/preceding ts to guard against isolated same-offset proposals.

findings.title: habit_journal onyx_tz_source ambiguously marks NY days as user_tz_log
findings.description: When a user_tz_log row exists and resolves to NY, tz_source is still 'user_tz_log'. This collapses ET-default and explicit-NY into one label.
findings.evidence: NEW.onyx_tz_source := CASE WHEN log_tz IS NOT NULL THEN 'user_tz_log' ELSE 'default_et_fallback' END;
findings.recommendation: Consider setting tz_source to 'default_et_fallback' for NY results unless an explicit non-ET entry was matched, or introduce a 'user_tz_log_et' label to distinguish cases.

findings.title: interval_to_tzd ignores seconds; could misreport rare half-minute offsets
findings.description: The formatter truncates to minutes, dropping seconds. While Garmin offsets are expected in whole minutes, the function contract is broader and could misformat unusual intervals.
findings.evidence: total_min := EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM diff)::INT / 60; mins := abs_min % 60;
findings.recommendation: Document minute precision or round before formatting. If future sources expose non-minute offsets, expand the formatter or validate input.

findings.title: daily_health_matrix_behavioral exposes onyx_et_date/local as behavioral
findings.description: The view sets onyx_et_date and onyx_local_date equal to the behavioral spine date s.calendar_date. This is fine if treated as convenience fields, but can be misread as true ET/local projections.
findings.evidence: s.calendar_date AS onyx_et_date, s.calendar_date AS onyx_local_date
findings.recommendation: Add an inline comment clarifying these columns are placeholders mirroring the spine date; consumers should use per-source onyx_* on joined tables for actual ET/local semantics.

findings.title: tz_log_gaps numeric conversion truncates seconds before dividing
findings.description: EXTRACT(EPOCH ...)::int / 60 truncates seconds pre-division. This is harmless for whole-minute offsets but brittle if inputs include seconds.
findings.recommendation: Use ROUND(EXTRACT(EPOCH ...)/60.0) to keep integer minutes while avoiding double truncation.

things_done_well.title: Centralized derivation with clear provenance and −6h rule
things_done_well.why_it_matters: A single function pds.derive_onyx_dates applies the bedtime-to-bedtime rule consistently and tags the source, reducing drift across triggers.

things_done_well.title: WHOOP cycle anchor implemented cleanly for Tier-2
things_done_well.why_it_matters: When available, per-cycle offsets provide authoritative local context without requiring manual log entries.

things_done_well.title: Transition-day surface area with an indexed flag
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Consumers can detect and treat TZ-change days explicitly, avoiding silent contamination of lag features.

things_done_well.title: Canary view distinguishes DST from real travel gaps
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Separating 'dst_artifact' from 'travel' prevents false alarms and guides targeted user_tz_log maintenance.

things_done_well.title: Behavioral matrix uses LATERAL to dedupe to longest sleep
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Selecting the main sleep per day yields stable, interpretable features that match intended semantics.

questions_for_followup: Should we broaden Tier-2 (cycle anchor) to the nearest cycle for waking-hour events within a capped window, to further reduce reliance on user_tz_log on travel days?
For garmin_hrv rows lacking start_timestamp, would you accept a provenance label like 'garmin_calendar_date' instead of 'default_et_fallback' to avoid implying ET derivation?
Do we already have an index on pds.whoop_cycles(start_time)? If not, can we add one to support pds.cycle_offset_for_instant and daily views at scale?
Is it desirable to standardize tz_source semantics when the resolved IANA is explicitly America/New_York via user_tz_log (distinct from ET fallback)?
Should the behavioral matrix omit or rename its onyx_et_date/local placeholders to prevent accidental misuse in downstream consumers?
